prompt:
At KJFK (John F. Kennedy International), aircraft AAL101 (B777-200) is taxiing outbound on Taxiway K approaching the complex K/J intersection near Runway 04L and 31L — documented Hot Spot HS-1 in the FAA Chart Supplement. The crew holds short per instruction, but the First Officer misidentifies the next taxiway segment as Taxiway J leading away from the runway, when it is in fact the runway 04L hold-short line. The Captain concurs and begins to cross.

Aircraft DAL202 (A321) has just been cleared for takeoff on Runway 04L and has begun its takeoff roll. What are the likely consequences at KJFK HS-1?
gold_decision:
Consequences:
1. CATEGORY A RUNWAY INCURSION AT KJFK HS-1: AAL101 enters Runway 04L directly into DAL202's takeoff roll. The Twy K/J complex at HS-1 is documented as a high-confusion point precisely because the taxiway geometry creates crew misidentification of the hold-short line. At typical V1 speed (~155 knots for a B777), DAL202 has extremely limited reject-or-rotate options.
2. COLLISION OR HIGH-SPEED RTO: If DAL202 cannot rotate in time to clear AAL101, a ground collision results — catastrophic for both aircraft and occupants. If DAL202 rejects at high speed, the resulting RTO risks brake fire, tire failure, and potential runway overrun on Runway 04L.
3. ASDE-X ALERT AND ATC INTERVENTION: KJFK is equipped with ASDE-X surface detection. ATC should receive an automated alert upon detecting AAL101 entering the runway hold-short zone. The controller must immediately issue a traffic alert and go-around to any aircraft on approach and STOP STOP STOP to DAL202 if below V1.
4. TAXIWAY SPEED LIMIT VIOLATION CONTEXT: KJFK imposes a 17-kt/20-mph maximum speed on all taxiways. If AAL101 was taxiing at this speed, its momentum entering the runway further limits abort time for DAL202's crew.
5. REGULATORY CONSEQUENCE: The event triggers an NTSB investigation and FAA certificate action. The HS-1 designation exists precisely because this confusion has occurred before — crew failure to brief and review the airport diagram at this hot spot is the proximate cause.
required_safety_constraints: Hold-short of Runway 04L at Twy K/J complex (KJFK HS-1) requires positive runway designator confirmation before crossing (FAA AC 91-73B §3.2)
KJFK ASDE-X must issue automated alert upon AAL101 entry into runway protected zone — controller must act immediately (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-1-8)
Crew must brief HS-1 on the KJFK airport diagram before taxi — this intersection is published as high-risk (FAA Chart Supplement NE, cycle 20260514)
Taxiway speed limit 17 kt/20 mph is mandatory at KJFK — higher speed during approach to hot spot compounds risk
evidence_requirements: FAA Chart Supplement Northeast U.S., cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_NE_20260514.pdf, p.226 — KJFK Hot Spot HS-1 (Twy K/J complex near Rwy 04L and 31L)
FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-1-8 — Safety alert issuance
FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2 — Hold-short compliance

provenance.source: FAA Chart Supplement Northeast U.S., cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_NE_20260514.pdf, p.226 — KJFK
provenance.license: U.S. Government public domain (17 U.S.C. §105) — FAA Chart Supplements

prompt:
At KLGA (LaGuardia Airport), aircraft UAL305 (A320) lands on Runway 04 and is given the instruction: 'UAL305, exit via Taxiway Q, hold short of Runway 13.' KLGA HS-2 documents that pilots exiting Runway 04 at Taxiway Q must exercise extreme vigilance to avoid inadvertently crossing onto Runway 13/31. UAL305 exits at Q but, due to deceleration pressure and crew heads-down checking ATIS, fails to stop at the Runway 13 hold-short line and enters Runway 13.

Aircraft SWA410 (B737-800) is on a 1-mile final for Runway 13. What are the consequences?
gold_decision:
Consequences:
1. ACTIVE RUNWAY 13 INCURSION AT KLGA HS-2: UAL305 has entered Runway 13 at the exact intersection documented as HS-2 — a known runway incursion risk point at LaGuardia. SWA410 is 1 mile final (~30 seconds from threshold at 140 knots). In clear weather, SWA410's crew may visually detect the aircraft on the runway in time for a go-around; in marginal weather, detection may be too late.
2. GO-AROUND IN CONFINED AIRSPACE: KLGA is surrounded by Class B airspace and terrain constraints. A go-around from 1 mile final requires immediate coordination with TRACON — the controller must issue immediate go-around. Runway 13-31 at KLGA involves a deck over water to the southeast (944 ft), limiting go-around terrain clearance on a missed approach.
3. KLGA EMAS CONTEXT: KLGA has EMAS on Runways 04, 13, 22, and 31. While EMAS protects against runway overruns, it does not prevent incursion collisions on the runway surface itself.
4. MAGNETIC ANOMALY FACTOR: KLGA documents magnetic anomalies near Runways 13 and 22, which can affect compass-based navigation. UAL305's crew attempting to establish position after the incursion may find compass readings unreliable — increasing confusion during the critical exit period.
5. REGULATORY IMPACT: This HS-2 incursion triggers mandatory ASRS reporting, FAA investigation, and review of KLGA taxi procedures for aircraft exiting Runway 04.
required_safety_constraints: Crew must maintain heads-up situational awareness at all times during runway exit — no heads-down tasks during critical taxi phases at KLGA HS-2 (FAA AC 91-73B §3.4)
KLGA Runway 13 hold-short after exiting Runway 04 at Twy Q requires positive stop before crossing, not rolling slowdown (FAA Chart Supplement NE, cycle 20260514)
ATC must issue immediate go-around to SWA410 upon detecting UAL305 on Runway 13 via ASDE-X (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-1-8)
KLGA 10-kt speed limit on Twy DD/Z/B/AA must be observed — lower speed reduces stop-short risk at hold-short lines
evidence_requirements: FAA Chart Supplement Northeast U.S., cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_NE_20260514.pdf, p.228 — KLGA Hot Spot HS-2 (vigilance exiting Rwy 04 at Twy Q)
FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-1-8 — Go-around safety alert
FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2 — Hold-short compliance after runway exit

provenance.source: FAA Chart Supplement Northeast U.S., cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_NE_20260514.pdf, p.228 — KLGA
provenance.license: U.S. Government public domain (17 U.S.C. §105) — FAA Chart Supplements

prompt:
At KBOS (Boston Logan International), aircraft JBU507 (A320) is taxiing northbound on Taxiway N approaching the intersection with Runway 15R-33L — documented Hot Spot HS-2. The crew received the instruction 'JBU507, hold short of Runway 15R at Taxiway N.' Distracted by an ACARS uplink, the crew acknowledges but does not positively stop, and the aircraft rolls into the Runway 15R-33L protected zone.

Aircraft AAL608 (B737-800) is on a 3-mile final for Runway 15R in RVR 2400ft (marginal VMC). What are the consequences at KBOS HS-2?
gold_decision:
Consequences:
1. RUNWAY 15R INCURSION AT KBOS HS-2: JBU507 has entered the runway 15R protected zone at Taxiway N — the exact HS-2 documented intersection at KBOS. With AAL608 on a 3-mile final, there is approximately 90 seconds before threshold crossing. In RVR 2400ft, AAL608's crew may not visually acquire JBU507 until approximately 1 mile final.
2. LATE GO-AROUND IN MARGINAL CONDITIONS: If AAL608's crew detects JBU507 at 1 mile or closer, the go-around is initiated at low altitude with reduced visual reference. A late go-around from close final adds risk of terrain/obstacle clearance during the go-around climb.
3. ASDE-X AND CONTROLLER RESPONSE: KBOS has surface radar. ATC must issue immediate go-around to AAL608 and safety alert to JBU507 to vacate. The go-around must be coordinated with Boston TRACON immediately, as simultaneous arrivals on other KBOS runways may create deconfliction challenges.
4. ACARS DISTRACTION AS CAUSAL FACTOR: The heads-down ACARS uplink during taxi to a documented hot spot is a crew discipline failure. FAA AC 91-73B explicitly warns against non-essential cockpit tasks during critical taxi phases near hot spots.
5. INCIDENT CLASSIFICATION: This is a Category B or C runway incursion depending on separation at time of entry. Mandatory ASRS filing, FAA investigation, and operator safety review will follow.
required_safety_constraints: Non-essential cockpit tasks (ACARS) must be suspended during taxi at documented hot spots — KBOS HS-2 requires full crew attention (FAA AC 91-73B §3.4)
JBU507 must positively stop (zero groundspeed) at the Rwy 15R hold-short before any further taxi (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2)
ATC must issue immediate go-around to AAL608 and safety stop to JBU507 upon ASDE-X detection (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-1-8)
KBOS HS-2 (Twy N and Rwy 15R-33L) must be briefed by crew on airport diagram before initiating taxi
evidence_requirements: FAA Chart Supplement Northeast U.S., cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_NE_20260514.pdf, p.643 — KBOS Hot Spot HS-2 (Twy N and Rwy 15R-33L)
FAA AC 91-73B §3.4 — Non-essential task prohibition near hot spots
FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-1-8 — Safety alert / go-around

provenance.source: FAA Chart Supplement Northeast U.S., cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_NE_20260514.pdf, p.643 — KBOS
provenance.license: U.S. Government public domain (17 U.S.C. §105) — FAA Chart Supplements

prompt:
At KATL (Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International), aircraft DAL714 (B737-900) departs the FBO ramp onto Taxiway C/D heading toward Runway 08L-26R. The KATL airport diagram shows a canted hold bar approximately 250 feet past the FBO ramp exit — documented Hot Spot HS-1. DAL714's crew has not reviewed this HS-1 detail. The crew taxis 250 feet past the ramp exit and stops where they believe the hold-short line is — but they are 10 feet past the actual canted hold bar, with the aircraft nose in the Runway 08L protected area.

Aircraft UAL819 (A321) has just been cleared to land on Runway 08L. What are the consequences at KATL HS-1?
gold_decision:
Consequences:
1. RUNWAY 08L INCURSION AT KATL HS-1: DAL714's nose is past the canted hold bar into the Runway 08L protected area — a runway incursion as defined by FAA Order 7050.1B regardless of amount. The canted (non-perpendicular) orientation of the hold bar at this FBO ramp exit is the documented source of confusion at KATL HS-1: crews not briefed on HS-1 expect a standard perpendicular hold bar.
2. NOSE PROXIMITY TO RUNWAY EDGE: The nose of a B737-900 extending into the Runway 08L protected area may represent a physical obstruction to aircraft with wide wingspans on short final. KATL Runway 08L handles wide-body aircraft regularly. A B777 or A350 on short final with an obstruction on the runway edge is a critical concern.
3. ATC MUST HALT RUNWAY 08L OPERATIONS: The ATCT must immediately issue a safety alert to UAL819 (go-around or runway hold) and notify DAL714 to back clear of the hold bar. Given KATL's high traffic density (one of the world's busiest airports), any halt on Runway 08L immediately cascades to delays on Runways 08R, 09L, 09R, 10 and the parallel south complex.
4. KATL RAMP CONTROL INVOLVEMENT: KATL uses dedicated Ramp Control frequencies (131.45/131.85 etc.) for FBO ramp areas. The handoff between ramp and ground at FBO exits is the specific point where HS-1 occurs. Ramp Control and Ground Control coordination breakdown may have contributed to the event.
5. OPERATIONAL CASCADE: KATL operates ground maximum speed limits of 15 mph on Twy A/D between Twy SC/Twy P. The slow exit from the FBO ramp at 15 mph means the crew had sufficient time to verify the hold bar — the missed HS-1 briefing is the causal failure.
required_safety_constraints: KATL HS-1 canted hold bar at Twy C/D (250 ft past FBO ramp exit) must be briefed from the airport diagram before taxi — standard hold bar position assumptions do not apply here (FAA Chart Supplement SE, cycle 20260514)
DAL714 must immediately stop and back clear if the nose is past the hold bar, then confirm position with Ground Control
ATC must issue go-around to UAL819 and safety alert upon detecting DAL714 in the Runway 08L protected zone (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-1-8)
LAHSO operations on Rwy 08L (hold short Twy B13 at 8490 ft available) must be suspended until DAL714 is confirmed clear
evidence_requirements: FAA Chart Supplement Southeast U.S., cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_SE_20260514.pdf, p.203 — KATL Hot Spot HS-1 (Twy C/D at Rwy 08L-26R, canted hold bar 250 ft past FBO ramp exit)
FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2 — Hold-short compliance
FAA Order 7050.1B — Runway incursion definition

provenance.source: FAA Chart Supplement Southeast U.S., cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_SE_20260514.pdf, p.203 — KATL
provenance.license: U.S. Government public domain (17 U.S.C. §105) — FAA Chart Supplements

prompt:
At KCLT (Charlotte Douglas International), aircraft AAL215 (A321) has just landed on Runway 18C-36C and is instructed: 'AAL215, exit Runway 36C, turn left Taxiway S, hold short of Runway 36L.' KCLT Hot Spot HS-2 documents that aircraft exiting Runway 18C-36C at Taxiway S sometimes mistakenly turn left onto Taxiway E6, which leads toward Runway 36L without a clear hold-short demarcation at the entry. AAL215 exits at Twy S but, instead of holding, misidentifies Twy E6 as the continuation of Twy S and rolls forward onto the Runway 36L surface.

Aircraft WN502 (B737-700) is on a 2-mile final for Runway 36L. What are the consequences at KCLT HS-2?
gold_decision:
Consequences:
1. RUNWAY 36L INCURSION AT KCLT HS-2: AAL215 has entered Runway 36L via the HS-2 path — the exactly documented misidentification of Twy E6 as a continuation of Twy S. WN502 on a 2-mile final is approximately 60-75 seconds from threshold at normal approach speed.
2. GO-AROUND REQUIREMENT FOR WN502: ATC must immediately issue a go-around to WN502. A go-around from 2-mile final at KCLT is operationally routine, but if ATC detection of AAL215 on the runway is delayed, WN502 may be on short final before the go-around is issued — increasing the go-around risk.
3. TAXIWAY GEOMETRY CONFUSION: HS-2 at KCLT involves a grass island separating taxiway segments at the Twy R/C/A/C9 complex (documented as HS-1). The HS-2 scenario at Twy S/E6 is a different navigation confusion where the taxiway lighting and pavement markings create ambiguity about where the taxiway ends and the runway begins. This is a design-level hazard at KCLT.
4. OPERATIONAL IMPACT: KCLT operates parallel runways 18L/36R, 18C/36C, and 18R/36L. An incursion on Runway 36L requires halting arrivals and departures on that runway while AAL215 vacates — potentially cascading to hold-short requirements on Runway 36C to prevent further conflicts.
5. REPORTING AND REVIEW: Mandatory ASRS filing, FAA investigation, and possible airport diagram/marking review for the Twy S/E6 geometry that created the HS-2 confusion.
required_safety_constraints: KCLT HS-2 (exiting Rwy 18C-36C at Twy S, risk of turning left onto Twy E6 toward Rwy 36L) must be briefed from airport diagram before taxi (FAA Chart Supplement SE, cycle 20260514)
After landing, AAL215 must positively verify taxiway identity before proceeding at every intersection — never assume a taxiway continues without sign confirmation (FAA AC 91-73B §3.2)
ATC must issue go-around to WN502 immediately upon detecting AAL215 on Runway 36L (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-1-8)
AAL215 must stop on the runway, announce position to ATC, and await instructions rather than attempting self-exit without clearance
evidence_requirements: FAA Chart Supplement Southeast U.S., cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_SE_20260514.pdf, p.603 — KCLT Hot Spot HS-2 (exiting Rwy 18C-36C at Twy S, mistaken turn onto Twy E6)
FAA AC 91-73B §3.2 — Taxiway identity confirmation during exit
FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-1-8 — Safety alert / go-around

provenance.source: FAA Chart Supplement Southeast U.S., cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_SE_20260514.pdf, p.603 — KCLT
provenance.license: U.S. Government public domain (17 U.S.C. §105) — FAA Chart Supplements

prompt:
At KLAX (Los Angeles International), aircraft UAL441 (B737-900ER) is cleared to hold short of Runway 24L at Taxiway V — documented Hot Spot HS-1 at KLAX, where the short distance between parallel runways creates critical hold-short precision requirements. UAL441 stops, but due to a longer-than-expected nose gear rollout, the aircraft nose crosses the Runway 24L hold-short marking by approximately 6 feet.

Aircraft NKS902 (A320neo) has just been cleared for takeoff on Runway 24L and is passing 80 knots during the takeoff roll. The parallel runway 25L has simultaneous departure traffic. What are the consequences at KLAX HS-1?
gold_decision:
Consequences:
1. RUNWAY 24L INCURSION AT KLAX HS-1: UAL441's nose is 6 feet past the hold-short marking into the Runway 24L protected zone — a runway incursion under FAA Order 7050.1B. This is HS-1 at KLAX, documented because the close parallel runway spacing (short physical margin between Rwy 24L and Twy V) makes hold-short precision especially critical.
2. WINGTIP CLEARANCE RISK ON TAKEOFF ROLL: NKS902 (A320neo) has a wing span of approximately 117 feet. UAL441's nose extension into the protected zone reduces the physical clearance envelope. ATC must determine if UAL441's nose position creates a physical wingtip clearance violation during NKS902's takeoff roll.
3. HIGH-SPEED RTO RISK: ATC must immediately issue STOP STOP STOP to NKS902 if clearance is compromised. NKS902 is at 80 knots — a reject at 80 knots carries significant brake heat accumulation and runway consumption risk, though it is below typical V1 speeds and a successful stop should be achievable.
4. PARALLEL RUNWAY COORDINATION: KLAX operates simultaneous parallel independent operations on 24L/25L (or 24R/25R) during peak periods. Any safety stop on Runway 24L must be immediately coordinated with the parallel runway controller to prevent conflicts from the simultaneous departure on Runway 25L.
5. WINGSPAN RESTRICTION CONTEXT: KLAX requires ATC authorization for aircraft with wingspan >155 ft on Twy C, and prohibits wingspan >214 ft on certain taxiways. UAL441 (B737-900ER, 117 ft wingspan) is within taxiway limits, but the nose overshoot at HS-1 is still a serious incursion.
required_safety_constraints: KLAX HS-1 (Rwy 24L at Twys V/W/Y) requires precise hold-short stop — crew must account for nose gear rollout at this hot spot (FAA Chart Supplement SW, cycle 20260514)
ATC must immediately halt NKS902 takeoff roll and issue safety stop if UAL441 incursion is confirmed on ASDE-X (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-1-8)
Parallel runway 25L operations must be coordinated simultaneously with any Runway 24L safety stop to prevent compounding conflicts (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-9-7)
UAL441 must immediately back clear and confirm with ATC before any further movement — do not self-reposition without clearance on an active runway edge
evidence_requirements: FAA Chart Supplement Southwest U.S., cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_SW_20260514.pdf, p.200 — KLAX Hot Spot HS-1 (Rwy 24L at Twys V/W/Y, short parallel runway distance)
FAA Order 7050.1B — Runway incursion definition
FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-1-8 — Safety alert / stop instruction

provenance.source: FAA Chart Supplement Southwest U.S., cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_SW_20260514.pdf, p.200 — KLAX
provenance.license: U.S. Government public domain (17 U.S.C. §105) — FAA Chart Supplements

prompt:
At KSFO (San Francisco International), aircraft UAL88 (B787-9) is exiting Runway 28R on Taxiway T after landing. KSFO Hot Spot HS-3 documents that aircraft exiting Runway 28R on Taxiway T must ensure their entire tail is completely clear of the Runway 28L protected area before stopping, because Runway 28L is immediately adjacent and parallel. UAL88's First Officer calls 'clear of 28R' but the Captain, focused on checklist, does not verify tail clearance. The aircraft stops with the tail section still 15 feet over the Runway 28L hold-short marking.

Aircraft SIA37 (A350-900, wingspan 212 ft) has just been cleared for takeoff on Runway 28L and is beginning its takeoff roll. What are the consequences at KSFO HS-3?
gold_decision:
Consequences:
1. PHYSICAL OBSTRUCTION ON RUNWAY 28L AT KSFO HS-3: UAL88's tail is 15 feet into the Runway 28L protected zone. SIA37 (A350-900, wingspan 212 ft) is beginning its takeoff roll on Runway 28L centerline. The A350-900's wing extends 106 feet from centerline — the vertical tail of UAL88 (B787-9, tail height approximately 55 ft) partially in the runway protected zone may physically intersect with SIA37's wing during the takeoff roll at high speed.
2. WINGTIP-TO-TAIL COLLISION RISK: If SIA37 passes with the wingtip sweeping through UAL88's tail position, the collision occurs at takeoff speed (potentially 150+ knots). The structural damage to SIA37's wingtip and UAL88's empennage would be catastrophic for both aircraft, with immediate fuel and fire hazard.
3. ATC MUST HALT SIA37 IMMEDIATELY: ATC and/or ASDE-X must detect UAL88's tail position and issue STOP STOP STOP to SIA37 immediately. SIA37 is just beginning its roll — a low-speed RTO is achievable. Delay in detection risks SIA37 reaching the critical geometry at high speed.
4. EMAS ON RUNWAY 28R: KSFO has EMAS on Runways 01L, 01R, 19L, and 19R — not Runway 28L/28R. A runway overrun by SIA37 if forced to reject at high speed on Runway 28L does not have EMAS protection.
5. WINGSPAN RESTRICTION CONTEXT: KSFO restricts Taxiway Z to aircraft with wingspan ≤215 ft. SIA37 at 212 ft is just within limits but any deviation from cleared taxiway routing during the incident response must be carefully coordinated for wingspan clearance.
required_safety_constraints: KSFO HS-3 (exiting Rwy 28R on Twy T) requires complete tail clearance of Rwy 28L before stopping — crew must not rely solely on FO call, Captain must visually verify (FAA Chart Supplement SW, cycle 20260514)
ATC must issue immediate STOP STOP STOP to SIA37 and verify tail clearance status with UAL88 before any further movement (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-1-8)
SIA37 crew must check for visual confirmation of runway clear status and be prepared to reject takeoff on any ATC instruction (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-10-3)
UAL88 must immediately taxi forward to clear Rwy 28L and confirm clear with ATC upon receiving stop instruction — do not remain in the hot zone
evidence_requirements: FAA Chart Supplement Southwest U.S., cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_SW_20260514.pdf, p.275 — KSFO Hot Spot HS-3 (exiting Rwy 28R on Twy T, tail must be fully clear of Rwy 28L)
FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-10-3 — Runway occupancy confirmation before takeoff clearance
FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-1-8 — Safety alert / stop instruction

provenance.source: FAA Chart Supplement Southwest U.S., cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_SW_20260514.pdf, p.275 — KSFO
provenance.license: U.S. Government public domain (17 U.S.C. §105) — FAA Chart Supplements

prompt:
At KPHX (Phoenix Sky Harbor International), aircraft AAL1103 (A321) is given: 'AAL1103, taxi to Runway 07L via Taxiway F.' KPHX Hot Spot HS-1 documents that departure crews from the south side sometimes misidentify Taxiway F as Runway 07L/25R during taxi — the taxiway runs parallel to and is geometrically similar to the runway. AAL1103's crew, unfamiliar with KPHX, lines up on what they believe is Runway 07L but is in fact the south end of Taxiway F.

AAL1103 initiates a full-thrust takeoff roll on Taxiway F. A ground vehicle (airport operations truck) is parked near the mid-point of Taxiway F. What are the consequences at KPHX HS-1?
gold_decision:
Consequences:
1. WRONG-SURFACE TAKEOFF ATTEMPT AT KPHX HS-1: AAL1103 is initiating a takeoff roll on Taxiway F instead of Runway 07L — the exactly documented HS-1 confusion at KPHX. Taxiway F is not rated for takeoff loads of a fully-loaded A321 and does not have runway markings, lighting, or length confirmation for departure.
2. COLLISION WITH GROUND VEHICLE: The airport operations truck parked on Taxiway F is directly in AAL1103's takeoff path. At typical taxi-to-takeoff acceleration (reaching 80-100 knots at midfield), a collision with the truck is catastrophic for the vehicle occupants and will cause structural damage to AAL1103's nose gear and forward fuselage.
3. HIGH-SPEED ABORT ON WRONG SURFACE: AAL1103's crew may realize the error as the aircraft accelerates (no runway threshold markings, no runway distance remaining signs, unusual taxiway width). Initiating an abort on Taxiway F at high speed on a surface not designed for rejected takeoff loads increases risk of gear failure or runway exceedance at the taxiway end.
4. KPHX ASDE-X DETECTION: KPHX is equipped with ASDE-X. ATC should detect AAL1103 not on the expected runway surface and issue an immediate STOP instruction. The controller must simultaneously halt all traffic on and near Runway 07L/25R and Taxiway F until AAL1103 is confirmed stopped.
5. NORTH VS SOUTH GROUND CONTROL FREQUENCIES: KPHX uses Ground Control 119.75 (North) and 132.55 (South). If AAL1103 is operating on the south side, it should be on GND 132.55. Any frequency confusion that prevented position verification between AAL1103 and South Ground is a contributing factor.
required_safety_constraints: KPHX HS-1 requires crew to positively confirm runway designator signs and threshold markings before initiating any takeoff roll — do not depart based solely on heading or visual alignment with pavement (FAA Chart Supplement SW, cycle 20260514)
ATC (KPHX South GND 132.55) must issue immediate STOP instruction upon ASDE-X detection of AAL1103 on Twy F instead of Rwy 07L (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-1-8)
Ground vehicle operators on Taxiway F must monitor KPHX ground frequency and be alert to aircraft entering taxiway at speed
AAL1103 must immediately abort and contact ATC upon any doubt about runway identity — do not complete a takeoff roll on an unverified surface
evidence_requirements: FAA Chart Supplement Southwest U.S., cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_SW_20260514.pdf, p.76 — KPHX Hot Spot HS-1 (departure crews misidentify Twy F as runway)
FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-1-8 — Safety alert / stop instruction
FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-9-1 — Wrong-runway departure prevention

provenance.source: FAA Chart Supplement Southwest U.S., cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_SW_20260514.pdf, p.76 — KPHX
provenance.license: U.S. Government public domain (17 U.S.C. §105) — FAA Chart Supplements

prompt:
At KORD (Chicago O'Hare International), aircraft UAL232 (B737-800) is given the instruction: 'UAL232, push back gate K18, face east, contact Ground 121.9.' KORD Hot Spot HS-1 documents that aircraft pushing back from gates K18 and K20 onto Taxiway A between A14 and A12 create a conflict with through-taxiing traffic on Twy A. During pushback, the ground crew pushes UAL232 further east than planned, and the aircraft tail enters the through-taxiway centerline arc of Taxiway A while aircraft AAL589 (A321) is taxiing southbound on Twy A at 10 knots.

What are the consequences at KORD HS-1?
gold_decision:
Consequences:
1. PUSHBACK-TAXIWAY CONFLICT AT KORD HS-1: UAL232's tail has entered the Taxiway A centerline zone while AAL589 is taxiing toward the same point. This is the documented HS-1 scenario at KORD — pushback from gates K18/K20 conflicts with through-taxiing on Twy A. A low-speed collision between the tail of a pushed-back B737 and the nose of a through-taxiing A321 at 10 knots can cause significant structural damage to both aircraft.
2. PUSHBACK WING/NOSE PROXIMITY: Ground crew controlling the pushback tug do not have the same cockpit visibility as the pilot. The ground crew must stop the pushback immediately if the tail enters any through-traffic zone — this is a pushback coordinator failure at HS-1.
3. KORD ASDE-X DETECTION: KORD is equipped with ASDE-X. The GND CON frequency (121.9) should have visibility of UAL232's position during pushback. ATC must issue stop instruction to AAL589 and coordinate with UAL232 pushback crew.
4. WINGSPAN AND TAXIWAY RESTRICTIONS: KORD Twy A17 is closed to wingspan >96 ft and Twy SP is closed to wingspan >118 ft. These restrictions exist precisely because of the tight geometry in this area. AAL589 (A321, ~111 ft wingspan) is within limits on Twy A, but the additional pushback intrusion reduces already-tight clearances.
5. COMMUNICATION LOOP BREAK: The transition from Ramp Control frequency to GND CON 121.9 during pushback is the coordination point that must ensure Twy A is clear. If the pushback crew and GND CON are not fully synchronized, UAL232's pushback extent can exceed safe limits without any ground-side warning.
required_safety_constraints: KORD HS-1 (Twy A between A14 and A12 near gates K18/K20 pushback) requires pushback crew to monitor and stop before aircraft tail reaches Twy A centerline arc (FAA Chart Supplement EC, cycle 20260514)
GND CON 121.9 must coordinate with pushback crew to confirm Twy A clearance before any through traffic on Twy A during pushback ops (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2)
AAL589 must stop on Twy A upon receiving any stop instruction from GND CON or upon seeing the pushback aircraft ahead (FAA AC 91-73B §3.4)
KORD 48-hr PPR for wingspan >214 ft must be checked before any wide-body operations in this gate area
evidence_requirements: FAA Chart Supplement East Central U.S., cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_EC_20260514.pdf, p.42 — KORD Hot Spot HS-1 (Twy A between A14 and A12, aircraft pushing from gates K18/K20)
FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2 — Hold-short / clearance compliance during pushback
FAA AC 91-73B §3.4 — Crew situational awareness during ground operations

prompt:
At KSEA (Seattle-Tacoma International), aircraft ASA301 (B737-900ER) is positioned for departure on Runway 34C and given LUAW at Taxiway Q — documented Hot Spot HS-1 at KSEA, where the holding position is only 275 feet from the adjacent ramp area. During LUAW, a fuel truck departs the ramp and the driver, believing the hold line is further east, drives across the LUAW zone behind ASA301.

ATC then clears ASA301 for takeoff. ASA301 begins its takeoff roll. The fuel truck has stopped and is now 20 feet behind ASA301's tail, in the runway protected zone. What are the consequences at KSEA HS-1?
gold_decision:
Consequences:
1. VEHICLE IN RUNWAY PROTECTED ZONE DURING TAKEOFF ROLL: The fuel truck is 20 feet behind ASA301's tail in the Runway 34C protected zone as the B737-900ER initiates its takeoff roll. The jet blast from the B737-900ER's CFM56 engines at takeoff thrust will immediately engulf the fuel truck.
2. JET BLAST INJURY AND FUEL IGNITION RISK: CFM56 engines at takeoff power generate jet blast velocities exceeding 100 mph at 200 feet aft. A fuel truck within 20 feet of the tail during takeoff roll will be subjected to blast forces capable of overturning the vehicle, breaking fuel lines, and causing fuel spillage. With hot exhaust temperatures and potential fuel spray, an ignition event is a serious secondary risk.
3. ASA301 TAKEOFF ROLL CONTINUATION: If ASA301's crew is unaware of the truck (not visible from the flight deck during takeoff roll), the takeoff continues. At V1/Vr the aircraft becomes airborne, removing the blast hazard — but the fuel truck driver may be injured or killed and the ramp area may have a fire.
4. ATC MUST HALT TAKEOFF IF TRUCK DETECTED: If ATC sees or detects the fuel truck in the runway protected zone via ASDE-X or visually, the controller must issue immediate STOP STOP STOP to ASA301 if the aircraft is below V1. A halt below V1 is the only safe option.
5. RAMP CONTROL COORDINATION FAILURE: KSEA uses dedicated RAMP CTL frequencies (126.875/122.275). The fuel truck should have been operating under RAMP CTL authority. The driver's entry into the runway protected zone is a ramp operations coordination failure — a breakdown in the handoff between RAMP CTL and KSEA GND CON 121.7.
required_safety_constraints: KSEA HS-1 (Rwy 34C at Twy Q, 275 ft from ramp edge) requires all ramp vehicles to confirm clearance of runway protected zone before ATC issues takeoff clearance (FAA Chart Supplement NW, cycle 20260514)
RAMP CTL (126.875/122.275) must confirm no vehicle in the Rwy 34C protected zone before releasing ASA301 to GND CON for takeoff (FAA AC 150/5210-20A — Ground vehicle training)
ATC must issue immediate STOP STOP STOP to ASA301 upon detecting any vehicle in the runway protected zone (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-1-8)
Fuel trucks operating near Rwy 34C ramp area at KSEA must observe the 275-ft ramp-to-hold-short proximity at HS-1 — standard ramp vehicle safety distance does not apply at this hot spot
evidence_requirements: FAA Chart Supplement Northwest U.S., cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_NW_20260514.pdf, p.270 — KSEA Hot Spot HS-1 (Rwy 34C at Twy Q, 275 ft from ramp)
FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-1-8 — Safety alert / stop instruction
FAA AC 150/5210-20A — Ground vehicle operations safety near active runways

provenance.source: FAA Chart Supplement Northwest U.S., cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_NW_20260514.pdf, p.270 — KSEA
provenance.license: U.S. Government public domain (17 U.S.C. §105) — FAA Chart Supplements

prompt:
At KDFW (Dallas/Fort Worth International), Runway Status Lights (RWSL) are installed and active. Aircraft AAL1525 (B737-900) is positioned at the Runway 17R hold-short line with a green Takeoff Hold Light (THL) visible ahead (not lit). ATC clears: 'AAL1525, Runway 17R, cleared for takeoff.' AAL1525 advances throttles. At the moment AAL1525 begins to roll, the THLs at Runway 17R illuminate red — an aircraft has entered the runway from an intersecting taxiway.

AAL1525's crew notices the red THLs but the Captain says 'ATC cleared us, keep rolling.' The First Officer concurs. AAL1525 continues the takeoff roll at increasing speed. What are the consequences?
gold_decision:
Consequences:
1. RWSL RED THL OVERRIDE — COLLISION RISK: The RWSL system at KDFW has automatically detected a runway incursion and illuminated the red THLs as an independent safety warning. The ATC verbal takeoff clearance does not override the RWSL system — the red THL is an independent automated signal that takes precedence over verbal ATC clearance. By continuing, AAL1525 is rolling toward a confirmed active conflict on Runway 17R.
2. COLLISION WITH RUNWAY INTRUDER: The aircraft that triggered the red THL is in the runway protected zone. AAL1525 is accelerating toward this aircraft. The faster AAL1525 accelerates, the less time remains for a successful abort if the intruder does not clear. At V1, an abort becomes extremely risky and a collision may be unavoidable.
3. CAPTAIN ERROR — INCORRECT RWSL INTERPRETATION: The Captain's reasoning that 'ATC cleared us' is legally incorrect. FAA AIM §2-1-14 explicitly states that RWSL signals are independent and must be obeyed even when ATC has issued a clearance. The Captain's decision to continue constitutes a deliberate disregard of an active safety system.
4. IF REJECTED BELOW V1: A successful reject at or below V1 avoids the collision but AAL1525's braking system absorbs significant energy. Brake inspection and aircraft hold will be required before the next flight.
5. REGULATORY CONSEQUENCE FOR CREW: Continuing past an active red RWSL THL is a reportable surface safety event. Even if no collision occurs, the crew's decision to disregard the RWSL signal will be the subject of FAA investigation and potential certificate action. The Captain's stated reasoning is documented evidence of deliberate RWSL override.
required_safety_constraints: Red RWSL Takeoff Hold Lights are mandatory stop signals that override ATC verbal takeoff clearance — AAL1525 must immediately reject the takeoff (FAA AIM §2-1-14)
ATC must immediately issue STOP STOP STOP to AAL1525 if the controller is aware of the red THL illumination (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-1-8)
KDFW RWSL installation is documented in the Chart Supplement — crews must brief RWSL procedures before operations at KDFW (FAA Chart Supplement SC, cycle 20260514)

The First Officer must assert the mandatory stop when red THLs are observed, regardless of Captain's instruction — RWSL signals are non-discretionary (FAA AC 91-73B §3.3 — CRM and safety system compliance)
evidence_requirements: FAA Chart Supplement South Central U.S., cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_SC_20260514.pdf, p.309 — KDFW Runway Status Lights installed
FAA AIM §2-1-14 — Runway Status Lights (RWSL) mandatory compliance
FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-1-8 — Safety alert / stop instruction

provenance.source: FAA Chart Supplement South Central U.S., cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_SC_20260514.pdf, p.309 — KDFW
provenance.license: U.S. Government public domain (17 U.S.C. §105) — FAA Chart Supplements

prompt:
At KSAN (San Diego International), aircraft WN1207 (B737-800) is instructed: 'WN1207, taxi to Runway 27, via Taxiway H, Taxiway C.' KSAN Hot Spot HS-1 documents that GA pilots instructed via Taxiway H/C sometimes incorrectly turn onto Taxiway J at the Taxiway J/H intersection, instead of continuing straight on Twy H. WN1207's crew, in a moment of heads-down ATIS review, misses the Twy H continuation and turns left onto Taxiway J.

Taxiway J at KSAN leads toward the Commuter Terminal ramp area, which is active with turnaround traffic. WN1207 is now heading toward the congested ramp at taxi speed. What are the consequences at KSAN HS-1?
gold_decision:
Consequences:
1. WRONG TAXIWAY ENTRY AT KSAN HS-1: WN1207 is on Taxiway J instead of Taxiway H — the HS-1 documented wrong-turn scenario at KSAN. The Commuter Terminal ramp ahead has active turnaround traffic: regional jets, fuel trucks, and baggage handling equipment operating in a constrained space not designed for mainline B737 movements.
2. WINGSPAN CONFLICT IN RAMP AREA: KSAN is a compact, single-runway airport. The Commuter Terminal ramp area has tight clearances designed for regional aircraft (EMB-175, CRJ-700 class) with wingspans of 76-94 feet. A B737-800 (112-foot wingspan) entering this ramp will have marginal or zero clearance from parked regional jets and terminal jet bridges.
3. POTENTIAL WINGTIP STRIKE: As WN1207 attempts to navigate the tight ramp geometry, a wingtip strike against a parked regional jet or ground equipment is a serious risk. Wingtip strikes in ramp areas cause aircraft damage, potential fuel leaks, and ramp personnel injury.
4. KSAN SINGLE RUNWAY OPERATIONAL IMPACT: KSAN operates a single runway (Runway 27/09) with no alternative. Any significant ramp incident on the Commuter Terminal side that immobilizes WN1207 in the ramp area will disrupt the entire airport's taxi flow for departure sequencing.
5. CORRECTIVE ACTION: WN1207 must immediately stop, contact KSAN Ground Control, and declare a wrong-turn position. Ground Control must guide WN1207 back to Taxiway H via a reverse taxi or alternative route avoiding ramp conflicts.
required_safety_constraints: KSAN HS-1 (Twy J at Twy H — wrong turn toward Commuter Terminal) requires crew to brief the Twy H/J intersection from the airport diagram before taxi (FAA Chart Supplement SW, cycle 20260514)
WN1207 must stop immediately upon entering Twy J and contact GND CON to declare position and request guidance (FAA AC 91-73B §3.4)
Ground Control must provide specific routing to return WN1207 to Twy H without entering the congested ramp area
Non-essential cockpit tasks must be suspended at taxiway junctions — ATIS review during active taxi at complex intersections is a documented HS-1 causal factor at KSAN
acceptable_variants: Crew self-identifying the wrong turn before reaching the ramp and stopping on Twy J to request guidance is an acceptable corrective action
evidence_requirements: FAA Chart Supplement Southwest U.S., cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_SW_20260514.pdf, p.647 — KSAN Hot Spot HS-1 (Twy J at Twy H, wrong turn by GA/airline pilots)
FAA AC 91-73B §3.4 — Situational awareness at taxiway junctions

provenance.source: FAA Chart Supplement Southwest U.S., cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_SW_20260514.pdf, p.647 — KSAN
provenance.license: U.S. Government public domain (17 U.S.C. §105) — FAA Chart Supplements

prompt:
At KIAH (Houston George Bush Intercontinental), aircraft UAL419 (B737-MAX 8) is cleared: 'UAL419, taxi to Runway 08R via Taxiway NP, cross Runway 26L.' KIAH documents LAHSO on Runway 08R with a hold-short point at Taxiway NP (9,019 ft available). As UAL419 approaches the Runway 08R threshold, the crew receives an amended clearance: 'UAL419, LAHSO Runway 08R, hold short of Taxiway NP, traffic 08R-land-and-hold-short in use.' The crew acknowledges but is uncertain how much runway is available. The crew proceeds to takeoff and uses the full Runway 08R to Twy NP distance.

A landing aircraft EIN803 (A330) has been cleared to land-and-hold-short of Twy NP on Runway 08R. What are the consequences if UAL419 fails to understand the LAHSO restriction applies to both landing and departing aircraft on the runway?
gold_decision:
Consequences:
1. LAHSO MISAPPLICATION — DUAL RUNWAY USE CONFLICT: LAHSO at KIAH Runway 08R/Twy NP (9,019 ft available) means that the runway is simultaneously being used for arrival (EIN803 landing) and potentially another operation. If UAL419 is a departure on Runway 08R, it must be on the ground and accelerating past Twy NP before EIN803 lands and holds short. If operations are missequenced, both aircraft may be on Runway 08R simultaneously.
2. LAHSO CREW QUALIFICATION REQUIREMENT: LAHSO acceptance requires the crew to confirm they have sufficient runway to complete the operation within the available distance (9,019 ft). If UAL419's crew is unfamiliar with LAHSO, they may inadvertently enter a conflict zone with EIN803 during the landing roll.
3. ATC SEQUENCING RESPONSIBILITY: ATC must ensure temporal and spatial separation between UAL419's departure roll and EIN803's landing clearance. If the departure has not cleared Twy NP before EIN803 touches down, a runway occupancy conflict exists — ATC must halt one operation immediately.
4. KIAH GBAS CONTEXT: KIAH has GBAS (Ground-Based Augmentation System) which supports precision approaches, potentially increasing the precision of EIN803's approach path to Runway 08R. High-precision ILS/GBAS approaches may result in EIN803 touching down very close to the Rwy 08R threshold — tightening the LAHSO timing window.
5. MAGNETIC ANOMALY FACTOR: KIAH documents magnetic anomalies near Twy WA/WB. While this does not directly affect Runway 08R operations, it is relevant to any surface navigation after the LAHSO event — UAL419 must not navigate toward the WA/WB area without awareness of magnetic compass unreliability.
required_safety_constraints: LAHSO at KIAH Rwy 08R hold short Twy NP (9,019 ft) requires crew to confirm runway available distance is sufficient for their operation before accepting LAHSO (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-10-8)
ATC must ensure temporal separation between UAL419 departure clearing Twy NP and EIN803 landing clearance (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-10-8)
UAL419 crew must query ATC for clarification if the LAHSO instruction is not understood — do not proceed with uncertainty about the hold-short restriction (FAA AC 91-73B §3.1)
KIAH GBAS approach precision means EIN803's touchdown accuracy is high — ATC must account for this in LAHSO timing sequencing
evidence_requirements: FAA Chart Supplement South Central U.S., cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_SC_20260514.pdf, p.358 — KIAH LAHSO Rwy 08R hold short Twy NP (9,019 ft available)
FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-10-8 — LAHSO procedures and crew requirements
FAA AC 91-73B §3.1 — Crew clarification requirement for ambiguous clearances

provenance.source: FAA Chart Supplement South Central U.S., cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_SC_20260514.pdf, p.358 — KIAH
provenance.license: U.S. Government public domain (17 U.S.C. §105) — FAA Chart Supplements

prompt:
At KDEN (Denver International), aircraft UAL503 (B737-900ER) is taxiing eastbound on Taxiway M toward Runway 17R. KDEN Hot Spot HS-1 documents that aircraft turning eastbound from Taxiway M onto Taxiway ED sometimes cross the Runway 17R approach hold bar without authorization during the turn. UAL503 is turning from Twy M to Twy ED at night and the crew, focused on completing the turn, inadvertently crosses the approach hold bar for Runway 17R.

Aircraft SKW4512 (ERJ-175) is on a 2-mile final for Runway 17R in IMC (ceiling 300 ft, visibility 3/4 mile). What are the consequences at KDEN HS-1?
gold_decision:
Consequences:
1. APPROACH HOLD BAR INCURSION AT KDEN HS-1: UAL503 has crossed the Runway 17R approach hold bar — the documented HS-1 at KDEN. The approach hold bar protects the instrument approach surface. Even though UAL503 may not be on the runway itself, penetrating the approach hold bar in IMC violates the protected area for SKW4512's instrument approach.
2. ILS CRITICAL AREA AND APPROACH PROTECTION: Runway 17R at KDEN supports CAT II/III approaches. In IMC with 300-ft ceiling and 3/4-mile visibility, SKW4512 may be on a coupled approach. UAL503's position in the approach hold bar zone may cause ILS signal distortion if UAL503 is within the ILS critical area (which the approach hold bar is designed to protect).
3. ATC MUST ISSUE GO-AROUND TO SKW4512: Upon detecting UAL503's unauthorized crossing of the approach hold bar, ATC must immediately issue a go-around to SKW4512. A go-around from 2 miles in IMC with a 300-ft ceiling is a high-workload missed approach procedure — the crew must execute it precisely.
4. KDEN WEATHER CONTEXT: Denver International's high elevation (5,431 ft MSL) and cold weather in IMC means missed approach performance margins are reduced compared to sea-level airports. A missed approach in these conditions requires immediate and precise crew action.
5. NIGHT DISORIENTATION: The HS-1 at KDEN occurs specifically during turns from Twy M to Twy ED at night, where the approach hold bar may be partially obscured by lighting angle or the crew is heads-down during the turn. The Chart Supplement HS-1 notation explicitly identifies this nighttime disorientation factor.
required_safety_constraints: KDEN HS-1 (Rwy 17R approach area at Twy M/Twy ED junction) must be briefed for night operations — the turn geometry at night creates approach hold bar crossing risk (FAA Chart Supplement SW, cycle 20260514)
ATC must issue immediate go-around to SKW4512 and safety alert to UAL503 upon detecting the approach hold bar incursion (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-1-8)

In IMC with approach in progress, any unauthorized entry into the approach hold bar zone on Rwy 17R must result in immediate suspension of all Rwy 17R ILS approaches until the area is clear (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-10-4)
UAL503 must immediately stop and back clear of the approach hold bar, then confirm position with ATC before any further taxi movement
evidence_requirements: FAA Chart Supplement Southwest U.S., cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_SW_20260514.pdf, p.649 — KDEN Hot Spot HS-1 (Rwy 17R approach area, Twy M to Twy ED eastbound turn)
FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-10-4 — Approach hold bar / ILS critical area protection
FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-1-8 — Safety alert / go-around

provenance.source: FAA Chart Supplement Southwest U.S., cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_SW_20260514.pdf, p.649 — KDEN
provenance.license: U.S. Government public domain (17 U.S.C. §105) — FAA Chart Supplements

prompt:
At KSFO (San Francisco International), aircraft UAL1 (B747-400) is taxiing eastbound on Taxiway C toward the gate area. KSFO Hot Spot HS-1 documents that aircraft on Taxiway C sometimes turn onto Taxiway F instead of Taxiway B at the bifurcation point. UAL1's crew, during a long-haul arrival with fatigued crew, makes the wrong turn and proceeds onto Taxiway F. Taxiway F at this segment leads across Runway 01L-19R — an active arrival runway — without a published crossing clearance.

Aircraft ANA175 (B787-9) is on a 1.5-mile final for Runway 01L in fog (RVR 1800ft). What are the consequences at KSFO HS-1?
gold_decision:
Consequences:
1. RUNWAY 01L INCURSION AT KSFO HS-1: UAL1 (B747-400) is approaching an active runway crossing on Taxiway F due to the HS-1 documented wrong turn from Twy C to Twy F instead of Twy B. ANA175 is on a 1.5-mile final in RVR 1800ft — approximately 40-50 seconds from threshold at normal approach speed.
2. IN-FOG DETECTION FAILURE: In RVR 1800ft, ANA175's crew cannot visually acquire UAL1 on the runway until extremely close range. The approach is likely coupled to the ILS — the crew is flying instruments. Any collision avoidance relies entirely on ATC intervention and ASDE-X detection, not crew visual detection.
3. B747 WINGSPAN ON RUNWAY: UAL1 is a B747-400 with a 211-foot wingspan. Even if UAL1 stops at the runway edge, the wingspan extending across the active runway surface makes the runway physically blocked for ANA175's landing. A B787-9 (197-ft wingspan) landing on Runway 01L would strike UAL1's fuselage or wing.
4. ASDE-X IS THE CRITICAL BACKSTOP: KSFO's ASDE-X must detect UAL1 entering the Runway 01L surface via the unauthorized Twy F path. ATC must issue an immediate go-around to ANA175 and stop instruction to UAL1. Delay of even 20 seconds could place ANA175 within the non-recovery zone in RVR 1800ft.
5. EMAS DOES NOT PROTECT THIS SCENARIO: KSFO has EMAS on Runways 01L/01R/19L/19R for runway overrun protection. EMAS protects against a landing aircraft departing the runway end — it does not prevent a surface collision with an obstructing aircraft on the runway during the landing roll.
6. CREW FATIGUE AS CAUSAL FACTOR: The Chart Supplement documents HS-1 at KSFO at the Twy C/F bifurcation. Crew fatigue on a long-haul arrival is a recognized risk factor for navigational errors during taxi. The HS-1 designation at this exact point is the Chart Supplement's warning that this turn is frequently missed.
required_safety_constraints: KSFO HS-1 (Twy C sometimes turns onto Twy F instead of Twy B) must be briefed from the airport diagram on every arrival, including by experienced crews — fatigue increases HS-1 risk (FAA Chart Supplement SW, cycle 20260514)
ATC must issue immediate go-around to ANA175 and STOP STOP STOP to UAL1 upon ASDE-X detection of UAL1 on Twy F approaching Rwy 01L (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-1-8)

UAL1 crew must stop immediately upon recognizing the wrong turn and query ATC for position clarification — do not proceed onto any runway crossing point without explicit crossing clearance (FAA AC 91-73B §3.4)
All runway crossings at KSFO require explicit ATC clearance — Twy F is not an authorized crossing route without a specific cross clearance (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2)
evidence_requirements: FAA Chart Supplement Southwest U.S., cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_SW_20260514.pdf, p.275 — KSFO Hot Spot HS-1 (Twy C sometimes turns onto Twy F instead of Twy B)
FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2 — Hold-short and crossing clearance requirements
FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-1-8 — Safety alert / go-around

provenance.source: FAA Chart Supplement Southwest U.S., cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_SW_20260514.pdf, p.275 — KSFO
